new_classes.definition_en: A spirit produced at a single distillery from a fermented mash of only malted barley, distilled in pot stills; classified under CN code 2208 30 30 (Chapter 22). Encompasses Single Malt Scotch Whisky as the prototypical example.

A distillation process carried out in traditional pot stills (batch distillation apparatus), as opposed to continuous column stills; the process by which single malt whisky is produced from a fermented mash of malted barley at a single distillery.
A quality of a spirit indicating that the entire production took place at one single distillery, as required for single malt whisky under CN 2208 30 30.

A quality of a fermented mash indicating that it is composed exclusively of malted barley, without any other grain or adjunct; the required raw material basis for single malt whisky under CN 2208 30 30.

coverage_explanation: The proposed axioms capture all four key distinguishing criteria stated in the legal text: (1) the product is a spirit (parent class eucn:Spirit), (2) it is produced in pot stills (PotStillDistillation process restriction), (3) it is produced at a single distillery (SingleDistilleryOrigin quality restriction), and (4) the fermented mash consists exclusively of malted barley (MaltedBarleyMashBasis quality restriction). The CN code 22083030 is anchored via a hasValue restriction on cnHeadingCode. A minor gap (score −0.05) is that the legal text specifically names "Scotch whisky" (i.e., a Scottish geographic origin), which is not modelled as a mandatory restriction here to avoid over-constraining the class beyond what is strictly required for tariff classification at this level — the CN code 22083030 covers single malt whisky in general, not only Scotch. If a ScotchGeographicOrigin quality were added, coverage would reach 1.0 but at the risk of over-specification.
